history.post_text:
Had to tell a senior engineer this week that his code reviews were killing team morale.

Not the technical feedback — that was solid. It was the tone. Dismissive. Impatient. Making juniors feel stupid for asking questions.

He had no idea.

Took him for chai, showed him three examples. His face changed. "I thought I was being efficient," he said.

Here's the thing: being right isn't enough. How you deliver matters as much as what you deliver.

We agreed on a reset. More context in comments. Assume good intent. Treat every review like you're teaching, not gatekeeping.

Two days later, a junior pinged me: "His review actually helped me understand the why."

That's the job. Not just shipping features — shaping how your team treats each other. 🛠️

#EngineeringLeadership #TeamCulture

pendingDraft.postText:
Most engineering teams using AI are optimizing for the wrong thing.

They're chasing speed. Faster code generation, faster debugging, faster shipping. But the real leverage isn't velocity—it's decision quality.

I've watched teams pump out features in half the time, then spend 3x longer fixing cascading problems because AI filled gaps in their architecture they didn't even know existed. The code "worked," but it wasn't right.

The engineers who are winning? They're using AI as a thinking partner, not a code factory. They ask it hard questions about trade-offs. They argue with it. They use it to stress-test their own reasoning before writing anything.

Speed without clarity is just debt with better optics. 🎯

#EngineeringLeadership #AI #SoftwareArchitecture
